Items.Title: 如何开始开源实习 | How to get started with Opens source internship
Items.Description:
开源软件已经存在了几十年，并且已经改变了技术行业。 开源项目通常由开发人员社区维护，他们在业余时间从事该项目。 然而，随着开源软件的复杂性和重要性的增长，越来越多的组织正在投资开源项目。 组织为开源做出贡献的一种方式是通过带薪实习。

带薪开源实习为学生和早期职业专业人员提供了一个独特的机会，让他们获得在开源项目上工作的实际经验。 这些实习对实习生和开源项目来说都是双赢的局面。 实习生可以学习宝贵的技能，从事有趣的项目，并为开源项目做出真正的贡献。 同时，开源项目受益于实习生的贡献，并获得新的贡献者，实习结束后可能继续为项目做出贡献。

带薪开源实习的好处之一是，它们允许学生和早期职业专业人员获得与他们未来职业相关的经验。 许多软件公司在其产品中使用开源软件，因此在申请工作时，拥有从事开源项目的经验可能是一项宝贵的资产。 此外，开源项目通常使用现代开发工具和实践，因此从事开源项目可以帮助实习生学习这些工具和实践。

带薪开源实习也有助于开源社区的多元化。 传统上，开源社区一直由白人男性主导，这导致开源软件缺乏多样性。 通过提供带薪实习，组织可以帮助吸引更多样化的候选人，这可以带来更多样化的开源项目和社区。

提供带薪实习的开源项目也可以从实习生带来的更多关注和知名度中受益。 带薪实习可以吸引以前可能没有考虑过为开源项目做贡献的人才。 此外，通过让实习生从事特定项目，项目可以更快、更高质量地推进，从而为最终用户带来更好的用户体验。

但是，付费开源实习也存在一些挑战。 例如，开源项目通常资源有限，支付实习生的费用可能很昂贵。 此外，实习生可能需要比志愿者更多的监督和指导，这可能会消耗项目资源。 最后，总是存在这样的风险，即实习生可能不像志愿者那样投入到项目中，并可能在实习结束后离开项目。

尽管存在这些挑战，但带薪开源实习对于学生和处于职业生涯早期的专业人士来说是获得实际项目工作经验、为开源软件做出贡献并有可能在开源软件开发领域建立职业生涯的好方法。 对于开源项目，带薪实习可以带来新的贡献者并有助于使项目社区多样化。 这对所有参与者都是双赢的局面，也是促进开源软件成长和发展的好方法。

如果您有兴趣获得从事开源项目的实际经验并为开源社区做出有价值的贡献，那么带薪开源实习可能是一个很好的机会。 以下是付费开源实习的一些示例，以及如何开始每个实习。

谷歌编程之夏：

该计划面向 18 岁以上就读于经认可的学院或大学的学生。 学生可以申请从事网络开发、机器学习、数据科学等领域的广泛项目。 要开始使用 GSoC，请查看参与组织的列表及其项目想法，并在截止日期前提交您的申请。

外展：

该计划提供实习机会，以从事各种领域的开源项目，包括网络开发、数据科学和软件工程。 要开始使用 Outreachy，请查看参与组织的列表及其项目想法，并在截止日期前提交您的申请。

微软开源程序：

其中包括 Microsoft 开源实习计划、Microsoft Research Internship Program 和 Microsoft Garage Internship Program。 要开始这些项目，请查看可用实习列表并在截止日期前提交您的申请。

红帽：

要开始使用红帽，请查看可用实习列表并在截止日期前提交您的申请。

该基金会提供多个带薪实习计划，包括 CommunityBridge 计划、开源导师计划和开放大型机项目实习计划。 要开始这些项目，请查看可用实习列表并在截止日期前提交您的申请。

要准备带薪开源实习，对您感兴趣的开源项目有深入的了解非常重要。 首先研究项目的历史、开发过程和社区。 加入项目的邮件列表或 Slack 频道，并开始作为志愿者为项目做贡献。 这将使您更好地了解项目的文化和工作流程，并帮助您在申请带薪实习时脱颖而出。

除了为开源项目做出贡献之外，培养您的技术技能也很重要。 参加在线课程或参加编码训练营，以学习开源开发中常用的编程语言、工具和框架。 构建您自己的项目并在 GitHub 或其他开源存储库上共享它们以展示您的技能和经验。

总的来说，带薪开源实习是获得实际项目工作经验、为开源软件做出贡献并有可能在开源软件开发领域建立职业生涯的好方法。 通过研究可用的程序并准备申请流程，您可以增加获得带薪开源实习机会并为开源社区做出宝贵贡献的机会。



原文:

Open source software has been around for decades, and has transformed the technology industry. Open source projects are generally maintained by a community of developers, who work on the project in their spare time. However, as the complexity and importance of open source software has grown, more and more organizations are investing in open source projects. One way that organizations are contributing to open source is through paid internships.

Paid open source internships offer a unique opportunity for students and early-career professionals to gain real-world experience working on open source projects. These internships are a win-win situation for both the intern and the open source project. The intern gets to learn valuable skills, work on interesting projects, and make a real contribution to an open source project. Meanwhile, the open source project benefits from the intern's contributions and gains new contributors who may continue to contribute to the project after the internship is over.

One of the benefits of paid open source internships is that they allow students and early-career professionals to gain experience that is relevant to their future careers. Many software companies use open source software in their products, so having experience working on open source projects can be a valuable asset when applying for jobs. Additionally, open source projects often use modern development tools and practices, so working on an open source project can help interns learn these tools and practices.

Paid open source internships can also help to diversify the open source community. Traditionally, the open source community has been dominated by white men, and this has led to a lack of diversity in open source software. By offering paid internships, organizations can help to attract a more diverse pool of candidates, which can lead to more diverse open source projects and communities.

Open source projects that offer paid internships can also benefit from the increased attention and visibility that comes with having interns. Paid internships can attract talented individuals who may not have considered contributing to open source projects before. Additionally, by having interns work on specific projects, the project can move forward more quickly and with higher quality, leading to a better user experience for the end users.

However, there are also some challenges associated with paid open source internships. For example, open source projects often have limited resources, and paying interns can be expensive. Additionally, interns may require more supervision and guidance than volunteers, which can be a drain on the project's resources. Finally, there is always the risk that interns may not be as committed to the project as volunteers, and may leave the project after the internship is over.

Despite these challenges, paid open source internships are a great way for students and early-career professionals to gain experience working on real-world projects, contribute to open source software, and potentially build a career in open source software development. For open source projects, paid internships can bring in new contributors and help to diversify the project's community. It's a win-win situation for everyone involved, and a great way to promote the growth and development of open source software.

If you're interested in gaining real-world experience working on open source projects and making a valuable contribution to the open source community, paid open source internships can be a great opportunity. Here are some examples of paid open source internships and how to get started with each.

Google Summer of Code:

Google Summer of Code (GSoC) is a global program that offers students a paid internship to work on open source projects. The program is open to students over 18 years old, who are enrolled in an accredited college or university. Students can apply to work on a wide range of projects in areas such as web development, machine learning, data science, and more. To get started with GSoC, check out the list of participating organizations and their project ideas, and submit your application by the deadline.

Outreachy:

Outreachy is a paid internship program for people who are traditionally underrepresented in the tech industry, such as women, people of color, and LGBTQ+ individuals. The program offers internships to work on open source projects in a variety of areas, including web development, data science, and software engineering. To get started with Outreachy, check out the list of participating organizations and their project ideas, and submit your application by the deadline.

Microsoft Open Source Programs:

Microsoft offers several paid internship programs for students and early-career professionals to work on open source projects. These include the Microsoft Open Source Internship Program, the Microsoft Research Internship Program, and the Microsoft Garage Internship Program. To get started with these programs, check out the list of available internships and submit your application by the deadline.

Red Hat:

Red Hat is a leading provider of open source solutions, and offers paid internships to work on open source projects in areas such as cloud computing, middleware, and more. To get started with Red Hat, check out the list of available internships and submit your application by the deadline.

Linux Foundation:

The Linux Foundation is a non-profit organization that supports the growth of the Linux operating system and other open source technologies. The foundation offers several paid internship programs, including the CommunityBridge Program, the Open Source Mentorship Program, and the Open Mainframe Project Internship Program. To get started with these programs, check out the list of available internships and submit your application by the deadline.

To prepare for a paid open source internship, it's important to have a strong understanding of the open source project you're interested in working on. Start by researching the project's history, development process, and community. Join the project's mailing list or Slack channel, and start contributing to the project as a volunteer. This will give you a better understanding of the project's culture and workflow, and will help you stand out when applying for a paid internship.

In addition to contributing to open source projects, it's important to develop your technical skills. Take online courses or attend coding bootcamps to learn programming languages, tools, and frameworks that are commonly used in open source development. Build your own projects and share them on GitHub or other open source repositories to demonstrate your skills and experience.

Overall, paid open source internships are a great way to gain experience working on real-world projects, contribute to open source software, and potentially build a career in open source software development. By researching available programs and preparing for the application process, you can increase your chances of securing a paid open source internship and making a valuable contribution to the open source community.

Items.Title: 学习真他妈难 | Learning is damn hard
Items.Description:
你想了解变革的阻力吗？ 你听过“千刀万剐”这句话吗？

想一想：学习新事物是非常痛苦的。

在每天使用 pandas 五年之后，去年我发现了 Polars 并决定学习它。 为此，我不得不强迫自己使用 Polars read_csv而不是等效的 pandas。 一开始我明显比 pandas 慢很多，每次我都问自己：“这次我可以只使用 pandas 吗？我很着急，我觉得这个新工具很慢”。 此外，保持在最前沿会让我自己暴露在细微的错误和不太明显的错误消息中，所以我自然会向上游报告它们，这会分散注意力。

一年后，我觉得使用 Polars 非常有成效，我认为这是值得的。 但是在学习的过程中，我感到很痛苦。

我已经使用 PyCharm 超过五年了，孩子们：在它被这样调用之前我就开始使用 Jupyter 方式（该死的：我什至在 2011 年左右制作了我自己的 SocketIO 驱动的类单元接口原型！） . 但是现在“每个人”（吓人的引号）都在使用 VSCode，所以为了更好地了解我的用户，我强迫自己去学习它。

男孩，这很痛苦！

每次我试图完成某件事时，我都会被 UI 中我不理解的东西卡住。 例如：右上角的“调试”图标完全忽略了自定义设置，您必须在另一个菜单中找到一个小三角形才能使用这些设置。 你知道我花了多少时间才弄明白吗？ 我可以继续。

本着良好的开源公民精神，我尝试向上游报告问题。 但有时你并没有真正的意愿或精力去做那件事。 每一次打扰都是分心，当你匆忙时，分心会让你愤怒。

我承认部分原因是因为我的学习过程。 也许我应该先看几个小时的视频教程，看看其他人如何使用该工具，然后开始研究它。 但是调整自己的学习方式，唉，也是一个费时费力的过程，一开始就觉得很惨！

我想从这一切中吸取一些教训，并想知道您对以下方面的看法：

善待学习者。 帮助别人学习。 创建实践社区。 教。 反馈是一份礼物。 对于每一条反馈，都有一千名用户沮丧地走开。

数字产品非常复杂，错误消息往往未经修饰，不一致的状态太容易达到，文档通常写得不好。 看别人做某事实际上可以节省时间，不要像我一样。



原文:

Do you want to understand resistance to change? Have you heard the sentence "death by a thousand papercuts"?

Think about this for a moment: learning new things is hugely painful.

After half a decade using pandas every day, last year I discovered Polars and decided to learn it. For that, I had to force myself to use Polars read_csv instead of the pandas equivalent. At the beginning I was obviously much slower than with pandas, and every single time I asked myself: "could I just use pandas this time? I'm in a hurry, and I feel sluggish with this new tool". Also, staying in the bleeding edge exposed myself to subtle bugs and not so obvious error messages, so I naturally was reporting them upstream, which became a distraction.

One year later, I feel very productive with Polars, and I think it was worth the effort. But during the learning process, I felt miserable.

Same thing is happening to me with VSCode. I had been using PyCharm for more than half a decade, and kids: I started using Jupyter way before it was called like that (damn: I even made my own SocketIO-powered prototype of a cell-like interface in 2011 or so!). But now "everybody" (scare quotes) is using VSCode, so to understand my users better, I'm forcing myself to learn it.

And boy, it's painful!

Every time I'm trying to get something done, I get stuck by something I don't understand in the UI. For example: the top-right "debug" icon completely ignores custom settings, you have to locate a tiny triangle in another menu to use those. Do you have an idea how much time it took me to figure that out? And I could go on.

In the spirit of good open source citizenship, I try to report issues upstream. But sometimes you don't really have the will or the energy to do that. Every interruption is a distraction, and when you are in a hurry, distractions make you furious.

I admit that part of this is because of my learning process. Maybe I should have watched hours of video tutorials first, to see how others use the tool, and then start poking around with it. But adjusting how I learn about things is, gasp, also a process that requires time and effort and feels miserable at the beginning!

Some lessons I want to draw from all this, and would like to know your opinion about:

1️⃣ Learning is hard. Be kind to learners. 2️⃣ Learning on your own is damn hard. Help others learn. Create communities of practice. Teach. 3️⃣ Sometimes people are in a hurry. Feedback is a gift. And for every bit of feedback, there were a thousand users that just walked away in frustration.

4️⃣ If people are resorting to video for learning is not because "attention spans", "kids these days", or any other boomer bullshit. Digital products are damn complex, error messages are often unpolished, inconsistent states are too easy to reach, and documentation is often badly written. Watching others doing something actually saves time, don't be like me.

Items.Title: 如何使用异步操作跟踪 setState 之后的状态？ | How to track state after setState with async actions?
Items.Description:
杨宇镇

发表于 4 月 15 日

要知道， useState函数是异步的。
所以默认情况下，如果您调用setState函数并尝试获取值，您将获得未更改的状态。
此外，即使useState函数是异步的，它也不提供Promise对象。
因此，尝试对状态更改进行异步处理令人头疼。

是的。 您应该调用“useEffect”函数并使您的回调函数在函数组件的状态更改后执行某些操作。

不过别担心！ 我有一个好主意。 让我们看看如何解决它。

准备一个具有Promise的Ref对象。

 const [getState, setState] = useState('') const promiseRef = useRef()

包装setState函数并返回Promise 。

 const setStatePromise = value => { setState(value); // your original set state function return new Promise(resolve => promiseRef.current = resolve) // Dispose when promise resolved. .then(() => { promiseRef.current = undefined }) }

调用useEffect并解析委托的Promise 。 不要忘记添加状态依赖以使Promise的 fulfilled 状态出乎意料。

 useEffect(() => { ref.current && ref.current() }, [getState]) // only for your specified state.

使用包装的设置状态函数并像往常一样进行异步工作！

 const handleSubmit = async e => { await setStatePromise('CHANGED') // do something more const response = await fetch('/path/to/submit', { method: 'POST', body: `myState=${getState}` // it will get changed state such as 'CHANGED'! }) const json = await response.json() // do feedback! }

让我们看看它是如何工作的：

  1. 准备一个ref对象来保存委托Promise的resolve函数。
  2. 创建一个包装setState函数的函数。 它的功能将使用现有的setState函数创建一个新的Promise对象，并将它的resolve函数加载到代理ref对象中。
  3. 在useEffect回调中，调用委托中加载的resolve函数使Promise状态 fulfilled。 状态更改后，您可以在等待下一个异步操作的Promise函数后获得更改后的状态。
  4. 在 wrapped 函数中，我还添加了then callback for perform delete the delegated resolve function，它处理了一个已经被 resolve 的Promise.resolve 。 此清理应减少内存泄漏。

这么棘手吧？
快乐的反应！

享受！

一旦暂停，组合将无法评论或发布帖子，直到他们的暂停被删除。

取消暂停后，composite 将能够再次发表评论和发布帖子。

取消发布后，所有复合帖子都将隐藏起来，只有他们自己可以访问。

如果复合没有暂停，他们仍然可以从他们的仪表板重新发布他们的帖子。

笔记：

感谢您维护 DEV 社区的安全。 以下是您可以用来标记复合材料的操作：

降低所有帖子的可见度

报告其他不当行为

Unflagging composite will restore default visibility to their posts. Unflagging composite 将恢复其帖子的默认可见性。



原文:

Ukjin Yang

Posted on Apr 15

You know, the useState function is asynchronous.
So by default, if you call the setState function and try to get value, you will get the unchanged state.
Furthermore, even though the useState function is asynchronous, it doesn't provide a Promise object.
So trying to do asynchronous works on state changes is making a headache.

Yes. you should call the 'useEffect' function and make your callback function to do something after state change in your function component.

But don't worry! I have a good idea. let's check it out how to solve it.

Prepare a Ref object that have a Promise. 

const [getState, setState] = useState('')
const promiseRef = useRef()

Wrap setState function and return Promise. 

const setStatePromise = value => { setState(value); // your original set state function return new Promise(resolve => promiseRef.current = resolve) // Dispose when promise resolved. .then(() => { promiseRef.current = undefined })
}

Call useEffect and make resolve the delegated Promise. Don't forget add state dependency to make unexpected Promise's fulfilled state. 

useEffect(() => { ref.current && ref.current()
}, [getState]) // only for your specified state.

Use wrapped set state function and do your async work as normally! 

const handleSubmit = async e => { await setStatePromise('CHANGED') // do something more const response = await fetch('/path/to/submit', { method: 'POST', body: `myState=${getState}` // it will get changed state such as 'CHANGED'! }) const json = await response.json() // do feedback!
}

Let's see How it works:

  1. Prepare a ref object to hold the delegate Promise's resolve function.
  2. Create a function that wraps the setState function. Its features will create a new Promise object with the existing setState function, and the resolve function from it will be loaded into the surrogate ref object.
  3. In the useEffect callback, call the resolve function loaded in the delegate to make the Promise state fulfilled. After state changes, you will can get a changed state after awaited Promise function for the next async action.
  4. In wrapped function, I also added then callback for perform delete the delegated resolve function, which disposes a Promise.resolve that has already been resolved. This cleanup should reduce memory leaks.

So tricky, huh?
Happy react-ing!

P.S. I made typescript function that do same thing as above. enjoy!

好的，现在，我假设你已经理解并完成了 JS 或 TypeScript 部分，它和往常一样。

现在，增压部分是，我们如何使用 Clojurescript REPL 来开始启蒙。

 pnpm exec shadow-cljs browser-repl

如果一切顺利，我们将受到 cljs repl 以及一个打开的浏览器窗口的欢迎，我们对 repl 进行的每一次评估都将在此浏览器窗口中执行。

 shadow-cljs - config: /private/tmp/x3/shadow-cljs.edn shadow-cljs - server version: 2.22.10 running at http://localhost:9630 shadow-cljs - nREPL server started on port 65047 [:browser-repl] Configuring build. [:browser-repl] Compiling ... [:browser-repl] Build completed. (119 files, 118 compiled, 0 warnings, 10.19s) cljs.user=>

这个 repl 与浏览器控制台的目的几乎相同，我们可以在其中键入有效的表达式来快速检查结果。 然而，这或多或少是一个强大的LISP REPL而不是 JavaScript 控制台。

有了这个 repl，我们可以实时做任何允许的事情，与实时运行时交互，调用所有可用的 JS api 以及实时修改它的每个方面，——无需重启、重新加载或类似的东西。

我们将使用我们自己可爱的编辑器（例如Emacs或VSCode）与此 repl 进行交互，从而获得最佳体验。 如果你刚开始，我建议设置Calva ，入门指南对新手非常友好。

对于这篇文章，我们将简单地与命令行 repl 交互以展示一些演示。

将此粘贴到 repl 中：

 (ns my.app (:require ["react" :as react]))

现在名为my.app的namespace已创建，我们的活动命名空间已从cljs.user移动到my.app ，如提示所示。

这个命名空间现在可以通过名为react的符号访问 react js 模块。 在称为interning的名称空间中将符号绑定到值的过程。

让我们尝试安装新的 npm 包，例如uid 。 一个小巧（130B 到 205B）且快速的实用程序，用于随机化固定长度的唯一 ID。

打开新的终端外壳并执行：

 $ pnpm install --save uid

回到我们的 cljs repl，我们现在可以更新my.app ns 来实习这个新安装的库。

 (ns my.app (:require ["react" :as react] ["uid" :as uid]))

现在我们可以访问uid模块，我们可以调用uid模块提供的uid函数，如下所示：

 ;; call uid function (uid/uid) ;; call it 100 times, return a list of 100 random uid (repeatedly 100 #(uid/uid))

接下来，我们定义一个名为new-rand-div函数，它将返回一个新的 HTMLDivElement，其中包含一个随机的 uid 字符串。

 (defn new-rand-div [] (let [x (. js/document createElement "div")] (set! (.-innerText x) (uid/uid)) x))

并测试调用以验证。

现在，让我们激活在 repl 启动期间打开的浏览器窗口，如果您检查该窗口，它有一个名为“app”的 div 元素。 我们将修改 dom 以展示我们如何与运行时交互以轻松修改页面。

 (let [x (. js/document getElementById "app")] (dotimes [i 100] (. x append (new-rand-div))))

验证浏览器窗口现在是否显示这 100 个随机 uid。

好吧，就到此为止。 我希望这足以证明我们是如何被增压的，即使只是一点点。

很好，节省一些资源，我们现在应该退出 repl 来停止它。

感谢您的阅读，如果您需要更多此类内容，请告诉我，我会抽出一些时间来创建后续帖子，这应该很有趣。

If not yet, first install the Java SDK on your machine, as well as node and pnpm itself.

You may have existing project that you can supercharge. As an example, here we start by creating brand new Vite project.

$ mkdir -p /tmp/hi && cd /tmp/hi
$ pnpm create vite@latest . --template react-ts

We now have a working project, verify with e.g. pnpm run build && pnpm exec vite preview --open.

Now, add shadow-cljs.

$ pnpm install --save-dev shadow-cljs
$ pnpm exec shadow-cljs init
$ pnpm exec shadow-cljs info

The last command will take some time while grabbing all the required dependencies. Be patient, after this, if all the installation goes right, all the doubts will be paid off.

Oh well, please don't feel intimidated. Those Clojure dependencies is much much more simple if you compare with all those in npm node_modules (e.g. pnpm ls --depth 15).

Ok, for now, i assume you already understand and done with the JS or TypeScript part, its the same business as usual.

Now, the supercharge part is, how we can have Clojurescript REPL to begin the enlightenment.

pnpm exec shadow-cljs browser-repl

If all goes well, we'll be welcomed by the cljs repl as well as an open browser window where every evaluation we put on the repl will be executed in this browser window.

shadow-cljs - config: /private/tmp/x3/shadow-cljs.edn
shadow-cljs - server version: 2.22.10 running at http://localhost:9630
shadow-cljs - nREPL server started on port 65047
[:browser-repl] Configuring build.
[:browser-repl] Compiling ...
[:browser-repl] Build completed. (119 files, 118 compiled, 0 warnings, 10.19s)
cljs.user=>

This repl is pretty much has the same purpose like the browser console, where we can type a valid expression to quickly check the result. However, this is more or less of a powerful LISP REPL instead of JavaScript console.

With this repl, we can do anything allowed in real time, interacting with the live runtime, call all available JS apis as well as modifying every aspect of it live, -- without restart, reload or anything like that.

We'll get the best experience interacting with this repl with our own lovely editor, e.g. Emacs or VSCode. If you just starting, i suggest to setup Calva, the get started guide is so newbie friendly.

For this post, we'll simply interact with the command line repl to show some demonstration.

Paste this in the repl:

(ns my.app (:require ["react" :as react]))

Now the namespace named my.app has been created and our active namespace has moved from cljs.user to my.app, as shown by the prompt.

This namespace has now have access to react js module via a symbol named react. The process of binding symbol to value within a namespace called as interning.

Lets try install new npm package, e.g. uid. A tiny (130B to 205B) and fast utility to randomize unique IDs of fixed length.

Open new terminal shell and do:

$ pnpm install --save uid

Back to our cljs repl, we now can update the my.app ns to intern this new installed library.

(ns my.app (:require ["react" :as react] ["uid" :as uid]))

Now that we have access to uid module, we can call the uid function provided by the uid module, like so:

;; call uid function
(uid/uid) ;; call it 100 times, return a list of 100 random uid
(repeatedly 100 #(uid/uid))

Next, we define a function named new-rand-div, it will return new HTMLDivElement with a random uid string inside.

(defn new-rand-div [] (let [x (. js/document createElement "div")] (set! (.-innerText x) (uid/uid)) x))

And test calling to verify.

Now, lets activate the browser window that is opened during the start of the repl, if you inspect that window, it has div element named "app". We will modify the dom to show how we interact with the runtime to modify the page with ease.

(let [x (. js/document getElementById "app")] (dotimes [i 100] (. x append (new-rand-div))))

Verify that the browser window now displays those 100 random uids.

Okay, that is for now. I hope it is enough to convince how we have been supercharged, even if only just by a litle.

Be nice, save some resource, we should now quit the repl to stop it.

Thank you for reading, let me know if you need more of this kinds, i'll spare some time to create follow up posts, which should be moar interesting.
